title: Defend Against AI-Powered Phishing

modules.title: Module 1: Why AI Has Made Phishing Alarmingly Convincing
modules.sub: Understanding the threat landscape shift AI has created
modules.description: Learners examine how AI tools have transformed phishing from obvious scam emails into highly polished, personalized attacks that defeat traditional warning instincts. They analyze real-world click-rate data and case studies to internalize why old detection habits are no longer sufficient. By the end, learners can articulate the specific capabilities that make AI-generated phishing fundamentally different from legacy threats.

modules.title: Module 2: Large Language Models as Phishing Engines — A Technical Look
modules.sub: How LLMs craft believable deceptive messages at scale
modules.description: Learners explore how large language models generate contextually appropriate, grammatically flawless phishing content and how attackers fine-tune or prompt these models for malicious purposes. They work through annotated examples showing the mechanics behind style mimicry, tone matching, and automated personalization. This technical grounding helps security professionals understand what they are defending against at a system level.

modules.title: Module 3: Spear Phishing, Whaling, and Voice Cloning Attacks in Practice
modules.sub: Recognizing targeted attacks on individuals and executives
modules.description: Learners study how attackers combine OSINT harvesting with AI generation to craft highly targeted spear phishing emails, executive whaling campaigns, and synthetic voice calls. They walk through reconstructed attack scenarios and identify the decision points where victims were deceived. Learners leave able to map specific attack variants to the high-value targets and organizational vulnerabilities they exploit.

modules.title: Module 4: Recognizing AI Phishing as an End User — Practical Detection Drills
modules.sub: Building personal instincts to spot AI-generated deception
modules.description: Learners engage in hands-on detection drills, evaluating a curated set of simulated emails and messages to identify behavioral and contextual red flags that signal AI authorship or manipulation. They practice applying a structured inspection checklist covering sender context, urgency framing, link anatomy, and request plausibility. By completing the drills, learners build repeatable habits they can apply immediately in their daily workflows.

modules.title: Module 5: Technical Controls — Filters, DMARC, and Email Authentication Explained
modules.sub: Configuring and advocating for layered email security controls
modules.description: Learners examine the technical stack used to intercept phishing before it reaches inboxes, including SPF, DKIM, DMARC, secure email gateways, and AI-assisted filtering tools. They work through configuration scenarios and evaluate the strengths and gaps of each control layer in the context of AI-generated attacks. Security professionals leave prepared to implement or advocate for these controls, while general users gain the vocabulary to ask the right questions of their IT teams.

modules.title: Module 6: Organizational Defenses — Policies, Simulations, and Incident Response
modules.sub: Building a resilient organization-wide anti-phishing framework
modules.description: Learners design components of an organizational defense strategy, including acceptable-use and verification policies, phishing simulation program parameters, and an incident response playbook tailored to AI-era attacks. They assess a fictional organization's current posture and recommend prioritized improvements across people, process, and technology dimensions. The module culminates in a tabletop scenario where learners lead or participate in a simulated incident response to a successful AI phishing breach.

modules.title: Module 7: Staying Ahead as AI Phishing Continues to Evolve
modules.sub: Sustaining vigilance as attacker capabilities rapidly advance
modules.description: Learners examine the near-term trajectory of AI phishing capabilities, including multimodal attacks, deepfake video lures, and autonomous agentic threat actors, and assess how current defenses will need to adapt. They develop a personal or team-level continuous learning plan that incorporates threat intelligence sources, simulation cadences, and policy review cycles. The module closes with a commitment exercise that anchors the course learning outcome to concrete next actions in the learner's own role.

synopsis: AI-generated phishing emails now pass grammar checks, mimic writing styles, and reference personal details scraped from public data — making the click rates on these attacks the highest security teams have ever seen. This course equips both everyday users and security professionals with detection instincts, defensive controls, and an organizational response framework built for the AI era.
rationale: Traditional phishing awareness training was designed for low-effort bulk attacks and leaves users and organizations dangerously underprepared for precision AI-crafted campaigns.
learning_outcome: Learners can recognize behavioral and contextual signals of AI-generated phishing, configure or advocate for relevant technical controls, and lead or participate in an effective incident response when an attack succeeds.
source_signals: How AI is Revolutionizing Mass Phishing Attacks and Escaping Detection
New Scams to Watch Out For in 2025
Cybersecurity 2025: Rising AI threats, new AI tools
